speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six. The liberator is Major Edward Ruston. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] When did you leave Neustadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it'd be the end of May, when the regiment was ordered home to reform in its true mountain role, to go to southeast Asia. And we made our way back by road transport, right across Germany. We-- our last camp in Germany was a place called [NON-ENGLISH], near [NON-ENGLISH], in the Rhineland, where we were-- we were honored to be visited by our old CRA of the 52nd Scottish Division, who came and had a look at us and talked with us and told us things which we always knew but nobody ever told us, that we were quite the best in the British army. And then we went, eventually, back to the north of Scotland, in Aberdeenshire, right up in the very north, a place called [INAUDIBLE] Camp, near Peterhead. And then from there, we went to Dingle, which is in Ross and Cromarty, in the north of the Highlands, to re-form. And we'd just got our first horses when the big bang happened over Hiroshima. And then Nagasaki. And the Japanese emperor had enough sense to pull the plug. And then there was no need for it. And so the regiment was disbanded. And the whole rundown of the British army started. And we had a release scheme. But I-- when the regiment was disbanded, I went back to Germany. I went to Bochum. I went, firstly, to the old German armored training area near Bad Lippspringe, with the artillery. And then I spent two years in Germany. My wife joined me in Rendsburg, on the Kiel Canal. And it was while I was at Rendsburg I got a visit by [NON-ENGLISH], who was the second in command to the Norwegians in Neustadt. And he invited me to the unveiling of a monument at Neustadt, on the beaches where these people were being murdered as they struggled ashore from the barges. That is where Mrs. Feniger tells such a poignant story. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Her full name? Saba. Mrs. Saba Feniger, who is the-- Where does she live?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is the curator of the Holocaust Museum in Melbourne. And she was there. And the-- then, in 1948, I returned to the United Kingdom. And, of course, I was now full-time serving. And then, in 1949, I was sent as a part of a mobilizing force to Hong Kong, when Chiang Kai-shek was being kicked out of China. And I was there for four years. And I must say, they were four very happy years. And then I came home from there. And I went to Wales, training young British soldiers, nine national servicemen. Very good lads, they were. Very good lads. And then I did my time there. And then I went to Northumberland. And I was seconded to a territorial army regiment which was re-forming after the shake-up in the territorial army. And I was with them for about 12 months. And I was then sent to senior officer school. And I did my course at the senior officer school. And the-- then I went back to my territorial unit in Northumberland. And then, all of a sudden, I was ordered to go and join a regular unit down at Pulford in the south of England. And eventually, found myself in that regiment, mobilizing to go to Malaya, jungle-bashing, chasing communist bandits in the jungle. But unfortunately, the Suez business blew up, so we got stuck in the Suez business. And there, to everlasting shame, the-- the whole thing was-- well, again, the Americans made nonsense of it. And we still haven't got peace in that area. But we could have had peace then. We could have that peace then. Because the-- the thing was finished. The Israelis-- which was now a state, which it never had been, during the time when I've been talking-- the Israelis got down to the canal. And the British and French commanded the canal. But the Americans said, no. And the Americans made the French and the British pull out. And that was it. That was it. And they're still arguing. I feel very sorry about it. Because it was finished. Nasser was finished. And it didn't suit the Americans. I'm sorry. I am pro-American, but I think they have made a balls-up of these things. I'm sorry about that. But that's what I think. I know that-- I know they've been good financial backers. But that was finished then. And Israel would have been free. And the Arabian states would have sorted themselves out. They'd had to done. Because they no longer had any power. And I'm sorry to say that it was President Eisenhower, for whom I have great admiration. He was my boss in Europe. But he made a cock of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reaction when Israel declared its independence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a realist. When the Americans refused to support the British exercising their mandate, which was a lawful mandate from the League of Nations and passed on by-- and to the United Nations, when the Americans refused to support the British, then it was quite clear that Mr. Ernie Bevin, who was the foreign secretary in Britain, was a very strong character. And Uncle Sam's dollars didn't frighten Ernie Bevin. And I-- I think that when you say they declared it, actually the United Nations declared the free state. I think that's the correct run of it. Mind you, I'm glad. They-- they've got to have a homeland. The-- I was very sad when they started taking it out on the British soldiers. Because, my god, we did a good job. We couldn't have done it any quicker. And after Dunkirk, we had to completely reequip. And then, of course, it wasn't until the Japs attacked Pearl Harbor that it was quite clear that eventually, the axis would fail.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did those first few post-war years compare personally to the pre-war years and the war years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, during the war years, we had an objective. And that was to win the war. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] There was a lot of difficulties in the post-war years. A lot of difficulties. To start with, Stalin was determined that he wasn't going to work within the full power agreement. He ran his own show in Eastern Europe, changing boundaries. The boundaries of Poland all changed. And he did it himself. The Yalta didn't really work out. But of course, he had this massive army and he was sitting there. There was absolutely nothing they could do about it. I-- I don't think you can compare one with the other. I think the greatest prize is-- what we've got to get is peace in the Middle East. Because that's at the crossroads of the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on a personal level, how happy were you in those post-war years, compared to before?

speaker: interviewee
text: How happy was I? I-- I've always been very happily married. I'm British by birth, Australian by choice. And I don't recognize that the British empire is dead. Although it's certainly changed. I think a lot of people-- I think that the English speaking nations of the world-- there's a map of them in the paper today-- the English speaking nations. It's a wonderful thing to have given the world this common language. So I'm very pleased about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally, well, I-- I'm happy enough, you know? but I'm sorry that there's all these bloody little wars going on by warlords. I mean, they're still talking about warlords. And, I mean, they talked about warlords [INAUDIBLE] boy in China. Chiang Kai-shek was a warlord. I tell you, it's-- it's nonsense. But, I-- my thoughts go to children. Is this a better world-- is this a better world than I knew when I was a child? And I think, quite frankly, it is. I think that there's more things. For instance, bring it back to Zionism. You have a homeland now. I know you-- there's still arguing and all that going on. But there is a homeland, which is established by United Nations charter. I'm right inside that, aren't I? And they never had that before. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I think children are better off. Because the great thing is communications. See, I left a sister in England. I left a son in Wales. And they're no farther away than my telephone-- when it's plugged in. I can dial the telephone, get straight through to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can write a letter here, go up to Middle Camberwell, give it to the postman, and he can fax it to a newspaper in London immediately. For $7. Well, what's $7? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of the things, I'm not very pleased about. Mr. Kidd, who I've spoken about, my headmaster, told us that you should do right because it is right. Now, I think that there are groups of people today who don't do what is right. They have ideas which in fact are immoral. They have ideas which ignore the rights of other people. I think we should all obey the law. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it's stupid what some of the things go on. But they are the frailties of man. The-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I-- I just don't understand some things.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the murder of Yitzhak Rabin. Dreadful. Absolutely dreadful. What good's it done? None. It's like the 35 people at Port Arthur. What good's it done? None at all. None at all. Now, they're going to have a lengthy trial. Dreadful. Dreadful. Can we still hear a few things about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've said that we are very happily married, which is true.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we've got three. My son, who is a psychiatric nurse, which is no mean task, in Wales. Daughter married around the corner, with two children, beautiful children. They're just [INAUDIBLE] back from Italy. They've been on assignment in Italy with their company. And then we've got another daughter, Sally, who is unmarried, who's in Adelaide. In a senior appointment. And then there's Cathy and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the names of the children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've told you our children's names. The grandchildren, we've got the granddaughter in England, [? Lenka, ?] who works for an American company. Had a letter from her today. And then, two around the corner, Toby and [? Katherine. ?] They're still at school, at school here in Melbourne. They're lovely. But all children are lovely. Mischievous, yes. But they're still lovely. And they deserve a decent life. But that doesn't mean give them everything they want.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left the army in 1958. The-- I served at the senior officer school. And although I say it myself, I got a good report, which would certainly take me up to Brigadier rank. But the army was immediately-- when I finished, the army was subjected to very heavy cuts. And in my regiment, they took a block of field officers and they drew a line at the top and a line at the bottom. And every name in in was told, you've got to go. So you can please yourself. And the line was drawn under my name. But I never objected. How did that affect you, though? I-- it really-- it's a bit shattering, you know, when it happens. A bit shattering. Because it's your career. Although, you-- of course, you always know it's going to happen, at some time or another. It just happens before you expected it, and while you still got problems of education and whatnot, a house purchased, and all those things. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if a family is devoted, they ride over those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Australia four years ago, on migration. But we've been here since '84 off and on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you come here permanently, what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: '94. February, '94.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Family's here. I want to see some of these children. And that meant a lot to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you like life in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, great country. Mind, it's a great country which I've just left. You know? Great, historic country. [INAUDIBLE] a great, friendly country. And it's given room to more people per acre than even the United States. That's a continent. And they've, of course, taken in millions and millions. But poor old UK, tiny little island, that there's a lot of people gone there, been very grateful for it. And a lot of your fraternity. In fact, it was Oliver Cromwell who came from my part of the country, who opened the doors to the Jews in-- in the 1600s, 1650 or thereabouts. They'd been shut since the 12-, 1300s, by royal decree. But it-- it was Oliver Cromwell who opened the door and said, yes, you can come.

speaker: interviewer
text: And within 12 months, one was a member of the stock exchange. They've got to make money, don't they? Or is it collect it? What do you like most about Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one thing which certainly I like, is that everybody speaks English. Australian, of course, mind you. I like their fierce independence. You must remember that I was alongside of Australians in the war. And I understand them. At least I think I do. And I like to be independent myself too. But I think the fact that all races here, whatever their race, whatever their color, they call all speak English if they want to. And if they arrive here not knowing it, there's facilities for them to learn it. So they have a common thing. And I think it's great-- it's great for business. It's great for social life. It's-- I like the weather. You never know who you're going to meet. I like the food. I like wines. I like the courage of the people. I mean, they may have floated Cairo down the Nile in the First World War, but that didn't hurt by being floated down, got a bit washed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about multiculturalism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it doesn't bother me. The-- it doesn't bother me. I really don't give it much attention. But I think since [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you'll never get harmony unless people can converse freely one with another, even if you don't agree. Voltaire who said, I disagree with what you're saying, but I defend your right to say it. I think, myself, that whatever the color, whatever their race, or anything else, they have this great opportunity here to converse in a common language. And I'm not saying that you're not allowed to speak your own. Because that's nonsense. But I think the fact that you can all converse together-- and I've been in schools here. And I had a great idea once that I would spend some time working with the underprivileged minority groups. But when I discovered those 8,000 teachers unemployed, I thought to myself, well, it's not a wise thing to go and offer your services free of charge. Might be misconstrued.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it-- it doesn't really worry me. I mean, I'm a member of the old British empire. And the Indians and the Sri Lankans as they call them now, and the Burmese, and the-- I've met them all.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've eaten with them. I've taken bread with them. And I've pinched salt with them. And it-- it doesn't make a scrap of difference to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your hobbies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I write-- write, now, in my garden. And do my best for my family. Because my great hobby when I left the army and finished my real working career was public duties. And I spent 20, nearly 25 years on various councils and-- in the United Kingdom-- and in statutory bodies, and, oh, a whole gamut of things, governing bodies of colleges and whatnot. I found it very interesting. But that is now history. But I still hear from people. I spent 15 years as a member of the Dorset War Pensions Committee. And our duty, our aim, then, was to help people who had been injured in war or suffering from the results of military service. When I say military, I mean naval, air, or land. I'm struggling to get them pensions or some sort of recompense. But-- that's-- that was very interesting. I was talking to you yesterday about that. But when a man gets a money order and said, look at this, I've never seen so much money before, then you feel all your efforts were worthwhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your military career teach you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't want to sound, you know, big-headed, but it-- it taught me [INAUDIBLE] everybody has their point of view, but at the end of the day, somebody has to make the decisions. And if you're in a team, then you owe it to the team to do your very, very best. And if you're the team leader, as I was right across Europe, then you are in a very privileged position. I-- I think that sums it up pretty well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in regards to what you experienced in the war, and regarding the Holocaust, is there, like, a mission you'd like the people to know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've done my best by writing down-- and I started this, actually, in Germany in 1945, when I was in Neustadt. I've done my best to make certain that, as I knew the truth, the truth was recorded. I've spoken up. I've supported-- well, I mean, it's-- I support a jury when I was in public office. There was a organization of women for Jewish organizations in Bournemouth when I was a county borough counsel there. It'd have been very easy to turn your back on them and say no. And I wasn't afraid to go into the street and, yes, I'll support you. And they were very, very appreciative. I've done my best, as I see it, you know. And I've been nobly supported by my wife and the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wish Israel well. And I shall be very pleased when they read Micah and put Micah into force. You know what I mean? Micah is one of the books of the Old Testament.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they can't do it until they feel safe. But I hope they will live in peace with the Palestinians. I really don't see why they shouldn't, in this day and age.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have a word for the survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Well, I think they are really the chosen few. But they-- the Saba Fenigers and those, they've suffered hell. And I hope that they live in peace.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't wish them more than that, can I? Because they will have the aches and pains of growing old, the same as I do. But if they live in peace, and they don't have to worry, I think that is a great thing. I think that they should be able to go to bed at night and not worry about having some louts painting signs all over their houses and kicking them and all that. And I think this applies to the other side as well, you know? I-- I think-- I just don't understand why mankind can't live in peace with his neighbor. I just don't understand it. Now, anything else? Can I show you my photographs? And my-- Thank you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my souvenirs. Because there's some of them that are very interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Major Ruston, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, thank you. Thank you for coming and I'd like to thank your-- your two chaps. I'll just say, shalom. May it be yours forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Likewise.
